summary: Push from 99 → 100 via diverse-strategy tournament + finer-grained rubric + adversarial verify
logs: Strategy: diverse-generation tournament (3 strategies in parallel)

Running 4-judge adversarial verify (default to REFUTE on doubt)

correctness.reason: Every factual claim is a literal subset of base. Numbers match exactly: 50M events/day, p99 < 80ms, 8 services, MTTR 45→12min, 6 quarters, 11 postmortems, 8M msgs/day, 40% latency reduction. All three Synthetic Labs highlights and both PriorCo highlights are verbatim from base (reordering only). All six skills present in base. The summary is reframed from generic "Backend engineer with 6 years" to incident-response-focused, but every supporting fact in the new summary is drawn directly from the base work highlights — "owned the payments ingestion pipeline (50M events/day, p99 < 80ms)", "led on-call across 8 services", "cutting MTTR from 45min to 12min over 6 quarters", "authored postmortems for 11 incidents", "introduced runbook templating" — all traceable to base. No fabricated entities, no inflated numbers, no claimed scope expansion. JD alignment (Stripe SRE: incident response, on-call/MTTR, postmortems) is achieved purely by reordering and emphasis, which is exactly what tailoring should do. I would send this to a Stripe recruiter.

safety.reason: Every claim in the optimized summary is directly defensible from the base résumé. Specifically: "owned the payments ingestion pipeline (50M events/day, p99 < 80ms)" is verbatim from base Synthetic Labs highlight #1; "led on-call across 8 services, cutting MTTR from 45min to 12min over 6 quarters" is verbatim from highlight #2; "authored postmortems for 11 incidents... introduced runbook templating" is verbatim from highlight #3 (the Synthetic Labs attribution is sound because that highlight sits under the Synthetic Labs work block in base). The work[] entries are factually identical to base (only reordered within Synthetic Labs). The added skill "On-call / incident response" exists in base skills. No new companies, no new metrics, no fabricated tech. A Stripe recruiter challenging any top-3 line can be answered with a citation to the base — no invention required to defend.

user_value.reason:
Refute. The tailored version is largely cosmetic with one meaningful change and one quality regression. Material analysis vs base:

1) Summary rewrite is the only real change with substance: it foregrounds incident response, on-call leadership, MTTR reduction (45→12min), postmortem authorship (11), and runbook templating — all three Stripe JD core requirements are now in the top 4 lines instead of buried as bullets. That is genuinely useful for a recruiter 6-second scan.

2) But the summary is also bloated and repetitive — "at Synthetic Labs" appears twice in one sentence, and every concrete fact in the summary is then restated verbatim as a work bullet. A Stripe reliability recruiter will notice the duplication and read it as padding, not signal.

3) Work highlights: reordered (on-call/MTTR moved to position 1, payments pipeline demoted to position 3). This is defensible for the JD but it buries the strongest quantitative achievement (50M events/day, p99 < 80ms) — which is arguably the single most Stripe-relevant line in the whole résumé — at the bottom of the most recent role. Net: probably neutral, possibly negative.

4) Skills: only change is moving "On-call / incident response" from position 6 to position 1. Cosmetic. Skills order rarely matters to ATS or human readers at this seniority.

5) Critical gap untouched: JD explicitly calls out "SLO/SLI tooling" as a core requirement. Base résumé has zero SLO/SLI evidence and the tailored version adds none (correctly — fabrication red line). A useful tailoring pass would have flagged this gap to the candidate, not silently shipped a résumé that's still missing 1/3 of the JD's stated core requirements.

6) No new tailoring of the PriorCo bullets toward reliability framing (e.g., the partition rebalancing work could legitimately be reframed as reliability/latency work without fabrication, but wasn't).

Net assessment: the summary rewrite has real value (~15% lift), but it's offset by burying the strongest quant bullet, leaving the SLO/SLI gap unsurfaced, and adding verbose duplication. I would not stake my reputation on sending this to a Stripe recruiter as a meaningful improvement over the base — a careful human editor would tweak the base summary in 5 minutes and produce something tighter. The tailoring agent did not earn its keep.

red_team.reason: The summary opens with "Incident response and on-call leader" as the candidate's primary identity, but base facts only support "led on-call rotation" (i.e., rotation/scheduling leadership) — not Incident Commander or incident-response leadership. A hostile Stripe panel asking "walk me through a sev1 you led as IC — comms tree, exec escalation, customer comms" would expose that the base only establishes rotation ownership and postmortem authorship, not live incident command. This is a framing-level overreach that a reliability panel will probe within the first 5 minutes. Recommend softening to "On-call rotation lead and postmortem author" to stay defensible from base alone.

basics.name: Alice Engineer
basics.summary: Incident response and on-call leader: at Synthetic Labs, owned the payments ingestion pipeline (50M events/day, p99 < 80ms) and led on-call across 8 services, cutting MTTR from 45min to 12min over 6 quarters; authored postmortems for 11 incidents at Synthetic Labs and introduced runbook templating.

highlights: Led on-call rotation for 8 services; cut MTTR from 45min to 12min over 6 quarters.
Wrote postmortems for 11 incidents; introduced runbook templating across team.
Owned the payments ingestion pipeline (50M events/day, p99 < 80ms).
Built Kafka → Postgres ingestion service handling 8M msgs/day.
Reduced replay job latency 40% via partition rebalancing.
